sentences.text: Yes.
It was-- It was-- and I mentioned that in the course of our stay, one of the sisters, Janina, sister of Masia, also arrived.
Also, she was taken to Pruszkow at some time.
But then she came.
She somehow survived this.
And she went to the pharmacy.
Then she was given-- somehow through a lot of all kinds of stories they had to tell, and she arrived in Bobrowniki.
And they told us that Warsaw was burning, and so.
So we were fairly safe in this village.
And at one time, we were-- yes, at one time-- one-- yes, so-- so Stefan was very-- Mr.

Kunat probably, he was giving-- he was getting from time to time, listening to remarks about maybe Jews, maybe something, too clever, too this, too that, because he was giving him very good advice, and he was listening to him how to improve his crop.
And they were very compatible, because Mr.
Kunat, a Polish peasant-- very clever man-- liked to listen a lot and be quiet.
And Stefan was a bit deaf in one ear.
So sometimes he was-- he was being silent and he listened to it, you know.
So this was like a conversation-- silent conversation.
But they get on very well-- got on.
And also, Stefan was very clever, because the village head-- head of the village-- decided that Stefan should be the head of the milk distribution.
So what was it?
They-- they had pasteurized that they-- a-- a plant.
Germans always installed in every place a pasteurize plant for milk.
Milk was gathered in big tins.
It was being brought to another-- not this village, another village, a main village-- was stored together, and then it was pasteurized completely.
The cream on top was taken for the German use.
And we were given the milk which was just fit for the-- for the pigs.
But because we didn't have enough food, we tried to use this milk, tried to boil it, to use it for-- and it was such a difficult process, because each time we tried to boil it, it curdled.
It didn't have the elements-- the chemical elements-- to-- to-- to be like a proper milk.
So that was a terrible fight.
But one night, we were sitting in our hut.
Yes, so we were given, once, an opportunity to-- to earn our-- our food by-- by digging potatoes for the great Count Radziwill.
Poland had many-- had a lot of aristocracy.
And one of the top aristocracy came, actually, from Lithuania, was Count Radziwill.
And he had a lot of land in this particular part.
And he allowed us to dig potatoes there.
He used to come in a big fur coat with a big stick and said, this you've forgotten.
This potato you've forgotten.
And this potato.
We are poor and carrying these potatoes like anything, and being hungry.
And so we said we teach him a lesson one day, because they give us like 30 kilos for the day of work.
So all the-- all the youngsters from the village, we gathered in the night.
And we devastated the field.
We took as many potatoes-- as many-- the whole young-- like-- and we took to our houses as many potatoes as we could get.
No wonder I have got a degenerated spine-- completely separated from the-- from arthritis.
And we brought it home.
And in the morning, they said, thieves from the neighboring village came.
And they took Count Radziwill's potatoes.
And we said, jolly good.
And one night we were sitting in our-- sitting in our hut with enough potatoes.
We were stealing the fat from the lady farmer, from the farmer's wife, and sausage, and whatever we could.
And she was oblivious of it.
She couldn't care less, and-- because it was supplied always to her.
And they-- they liked Stefan in there, because he always made like that one of the farmers, or two, skipped-- he skipped them.
So that he took the milk, all the milk with the good, so they could make butter for them.
So you see, it was a safe man.
So another one, he skipped.
In this way, he helped the hardship of the farmers.
And the Germans didn't know about it, of course.
But one night, we were sitting in our room-- there was only one room full of mushrooms.
And we suddenly heard Mr.
Kunat shouting on top of his voice.
Stefan!
Stefan!
Come out!
Come out!
Everybody come out!
We looked at each other.
We said we are seeing each other the last time in our lives.
The Germans are outside.
From time to time in this village, there were situations like that.
And we said that is-- that is we survived till now.
We should say goodbye.
We came out of the village-- of the room, and there was Kunat, animated, gesticulating.
Come to the shed!
The pig-- they had an enormous pig, which we called Marta-- the pig was in labor, having piglets.
And one was stuck.
And Stefan had to help.
So it was-- so I was holding the ladder.
Masia was helping Stefan with the piglets.
Kunat was there holding her pink head.
The farmers' wives all said that they-- it was a picture of unbelievable--
So realistic.
And we saved this piglet.
He saved it, because he was-- he was a very skilled man, Stefan.
He saved the piglet.
She had about six or seven.
And you should see that pig's face.
I tell you, it was more human than human.
The gratefulness on this pig's face-- how she was grateful for that.
So we didn't die.
We survived it.
And then one beautiful day in April-- late April-- somebody came, animated very much, gesticulating, shouting.
And we all run out of the-- of the hut.
And that was then a woman saying, the Russians are coming.
The Russians are coming.
The Russians are coming.
But-- Which mean?
Yes.
Was it-- I just-- the Russians are coming, with big tanks.
But-- Means the end of the war.
The end of the war.
But before that, I skipped one incident, which was-- I wanted quite quickly to tell you.
You know, during this period, before the Russians appeared, and we were still-- we were about a year in this village, there was a period there was very little food.
They-- they didn't have enough food.
All was taken by the Germans.
And it was quite a starvation.
And we-- about 20 people of us-- decided to go to Lowicz.

Lowicz which was like 20 kilometers ahead-- and to try to search for bread and for-- for vegetables, for whatever-- eggs, whatever we could put, for the starving children in the village, the Polish children.
So we all set out.
Stefan was at home.
He was much older than us.
Masia, and I, Janina, and all of the Polish young friends went to Lowicz.
And as we went there-- Lowicz was a small town.
I remember it vaguely now.
Now it's different, probably.
Completely different it looks.
One part of that-- of that-- it was a small city, not a village-- was, you know, like the front houses.
And in the back, were gardens.
And there were-- [INAUDIBLE] the marketplace, and very colorful place.
It was very quite well-known.
People used to-- villages sent all their goods there, and their dairy products, and-- and sort of poultry, and-- and animals.
And so we-- we purchased a lot of things, some sausages.
People were sorry for the-- for the children.
So we got [INAUDIBLE] good.
I remember eggs and milk.
And I was wearing a blue coat, which a German gave it to me, because I had-- it was-- I had nothing to wear.
And God knows what we gathered-- bread and everything.
And as we were returning to the fields, suddenly we heard-- we heard the aeroplanes.
And we didn't realize which aeroplanes it were-- Germans, Russian.
Apparently, it were Russian aeroplanes, which were forwarding in front of the Russian offensive.
They were already chasing the Germans, you know.
And it was-- there were ditches.
Everywhere there were ditches.
They would dig dig ditches.
So with the milk, with the eggs, with everything, I fell into the ditch, and all the eggs and milk on my coat.
And we lost each other.
I fell into ditch with-- with Masia.
I did not see Janina.
I didn't see lots of people.
And they were bombarding the-- covering the whole place with-- with-- with-- with-- with shells, because they were-- it was a real what you will call a raid-- a proper raid by the Russians.
And as they-- it was a big German regiment, 1, 2, 3.
And as-- [INAUDIBLE] when the [INAUDIBLE] seen that this is an air raid, they begin to run away.
So they started running away.
And this was-- this was-- God almighty, really, God almighty allowed us to see all.
We are in the trenches in the back and the Germans running, with motor cars, with horses, with-- with bicycles, whatever they could get.
And the fear on their faces.
You liked it.
Was the revenge of my life-- the fear.
The fear of them was far greater than the fear of the poor victims who went to perish in the gas chambers, believe me.
The powerful Germans, the lords of-- almost feel like it being God-- they were in such a panic, in such a panic, in such a fearful panic.
Behave like real cowards, running through the little bridge.
And we have seen it all.
And they were falling off, going up again, thrown off about on top of them.
And one on top of the other.
It was a spectacle, which delighted all of us.
And running-- but we could have been killed, because it was just showering us.
Somehow it lasted maybe seven hours.
And the Germans ran.
Some of them hid in hay stacks.
Afterwards, you know, when the Russians came after that, they killed hundreds of Germans, because they hid in hay stacks.
They hid in houses.
They hid in-- in every possible place.
And they hanged them on every single tree.
There was a big avenue of beautiful poplars.
And off every single tree, there was a hanging German.
They were merciless, the Poles, for what they have done to Poland.
But anyway, we return, somehow, safely, all of us, without any of us being killed, which we could have been.
We were just exposed to the-- to the-- to the bullets.
And we returned to the village.
And it was a great rejoicement.
And after-- soon after that, somebody came, you know, after a week or two, saying that.
And then the Russians came.
And first I remember running.
We had on a hill, little slightly, on a hill-- no, no, no, it's still-- it's still before that.
It's still before that.
Yes, yes.
And on the hill-- that's quite right-- we saw the first tank.
And I tell you, the tanks were so enormous.
People say that some tanks, they're built of tin specially to frighten the Germans, but they were not real.
But this was a real tank-- enormous tank.
And right on top of the tank was sitting a young Soviet soldier with his you know-- smiling and beaming.
And we know--
--we knew they'd come.
The war was not over, because they were going all to Berlin.

And then it was a very nice story, because they all were stationed at the farmers' places-- the oncoming Russian Army, which you know stopped in Vistula and refused to help the Poles in the uprising and then joining them in the defeat of Germans.
They eventually-- it was 1945, winter.
They cross the Vistula.
We were about a year in the village.
They cross the Vistula in 1945-- January, February.
And in a great speed, they came forward chasing the Germans.
So they-- they-- that's how the Germans were chased.
And then-- so the-- in every-- the Russians walked, walked, walked-- masses of them.
And then every night, they stop at another farmer's place.
So it's like 14 soldiers stopped at Mr.
Kunat's place.
And they brought a lot of vodka, food.
They took from every farmer food.
There was-- they were they had to feed them.
They nearly starved the Poland with the Russian army.
And they begin to wash their hands.
So they wash their friends.
So we said to ourselves, funny, mostly Russians, Asians-- mostly Asian people are in the Russian Army.
We knew there are Muslim Republics in the-- in the-- in the Russian-- Soviet Russia.
And many of them-- Uzbekistan, Tajikistan, Kazakhstan-- but there were so many, you couldn't believe it.
Anyway, they asked for water.
They start washing their-- No water-- this went to the pump.
You know, pump.
And they pumped they wash their-- so after 20 minutes, you have seen they were white.
But they were dirty from the battles.
They never washed for weeks on end.
And they were Asian soldiers with the-- Then they made their feast.
So they ask for-- they killed, you know, lamb or-- not lamb.
They were not so much lamb.
They killed-- they killed small calves and goats and whatever, and chickens, and poultry.
And they brought skewers with them from the lorries.
They had big lorries.
They had their equipment-- very big skewers.
They had-- they had somewhere to sleep also.
They erected some tents.
Big skewers, which were like one to that, and they were supplied-- they had to be supplied vegetables and onions.
And then they had this liver and lots of meat.
And then they put tomato and onion and mushroom.
You know--
On the skewer-- a big grill.
They made enormous fires-- enormous fires in the yard.
Then they put hundreds, hundreds of potatoes under the fire, and the skewers on top of it.
And they had enormous amount of vodka.
So they drank vodka.
They sat around the fire.
And they sang.
And you know, this was a picture.
It was a heavenly picture-- young men sitting around a fire, drinking and eating, waiting for the meat to-- to grill, and singing.
The sky was blue.
It was still light.
The sky was blue.
And these voices were heavenly voices.
They sang so beautifully that, you know, it was-- it was unbelievable what was happening-- the war, the Germans, the killings, the things.
And we were invited to it, but we were afraid-- so Stefan and Masia, and I, and Janina, we were afraid.
But we had to join a little bit.
We were given a little meat and a little vodka, which we were obliged to drink.
And then that's how lasted every day, something like that, because they were marching and marching for Berlin.
You know, chasing the Germans.
And when one batch-- the first batch-- came, it was all right.
But after a day-- maybe it was a day or two or three-- was a little bit Poles.
Then the other-- another-- number of soldiers came.
They devastate that village with the food.
And we watched-- and some of them, when they were in a nice, jolly mood, they just rolled up their hand, and there were watches-- one, one, one, one.
From here to there, were watches-- here to there-- women's watches, men's watches, whatever they could.
Took from people.
And when they left, we were missing all the watches in the-- in the-- Mr.
Kunat was missing his-- his clocks and everything.
But they behaved nicely towards us.
So we were liberated.
That was then.
It was 1945.
And I remember in 1945, I didn't yet know that it's the end of the-- we were liberated, but it was not the end of the war, because the Germans were going on Berlin.
I went to Warsaw, because I was going to be in touch with some-- somebody in Warsaw that I knew.
And it was about seven kilometers.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No, I remember it was something different.

They was very anxious to go to our villa, which was in [POLISH], which was completely different part, because I thought maybe my brother, maybe my-- maybe Rosa, maybe my father, maybe somebody will be there, because the villa was rented during the war.
Maybe somebody came.
If anyplace, that should be there, because we're-- so we made the journey, I remember.
And also, they were going to Warsaw-- Stefan and other people.
And we went the journey to Warsaw.
And it-- it was-- it was unbelievable.
And we sat like people sit on the-- like they sit in the David's psalm and-- and weeping over the-- over the the devastation of Jerusalem.
That was like that.
It was completely annihilated, torn to pieces.
We arrived.
We came eventually to the villa after a few good weeks.
Nobody there came.
I looked and never-- then I found out about my aunt who survived.
And then I got in touch.
She told me she got in touch--
Yeah.
She survived, yes.
Eva.
Yes, she survived.
She survived there.
Then she joined another who came in.
Anyway, she survived.
And she told me she wrote to my uncle in Palestine that out of the whole family, we two of us survived.
Because she knew already--
I had a few uncles, and a few aunties and cousins.
And she wrote to him.
And she give him the address.
And in the course of time-- and I told her where I am in that village-- and in course of time she let me know my uncle wrote to her to say that my sister survived, and is in India.
My sister survived the war.
She made a different journey completely.
And is in India.
In India.
And I was afterwards trying to contact her.
It's all of incidents and stories.
But I think that' it, that I will end about that.
There is still plenty to say.
But that will be the end of this.
So then I went to Warsaw.
I found my relatives by chance.
I didn't know the cousin who helped me, who was [? evacuated ?] before the war.
He was [? made also ?] [INAUDIBLE] He obtained a fantastic state job to rebuild Warsaw.
And I met two other cousins who wanted to take me to Israel.
But I didn't go to Israel, because I got in touch with my sister.
And she said to wait.
In Warsaw I will get you a visa to India.
Through my auntie who wrote to Israel, to Palestine.
And my uncle in Palestine was in touch with my sister in India.
Sister With your sister.
So that's how you got the address.
Yes.
That's how I-- yeah.
And my sister survived through another story.
I was one year there.
Took one year.
In Warsaw.
And I worked partly with this friend of mine who give me shelter after I have been with my cousin.
It was a newspaper--
I lived with a-- with a-- I lived with the Rzedowski family.
This cousin who's helped me during the war-- twice, not once-- who I met on the train.
And I lived with him and his mother about one year.
And then his brothers came from Russia, told us all kinds of stories.
And then, my sister obtained a visa for me.
Very-- she went through a terrific strain, and had to travel to Delhi and had to convince the high commissioner-- the English High Commissioner then.
And then-- and then the Indian High Commission, because the English were living already in India-- and obtained a visa for me to go to India.
And I went to India in '94, May '94.
Said goodbye to my friends, goodbye to my cousins, who were very kind to me and very nice.
And I went on my new life to meet my-- to-- to-- to start a new life with my sister in Bombay.
It was in May '46.
I went through-- through Sweden.
I was in Sweden for a few months.
And I was in Amsterdam.
Then I had boarded a KLM plane, traveled for three days to-- we passed Rome and Cairo.
At the famous Shepherd hotel we were.
Then we went to Basra, Karachi, and then to India, and met my sister in Bombay.
And my husband-- you see, that was like that-- she lived in Warsaw, of course.
Then when the war started, the Polish authorities over the radio said, every young person who can possibly should run away to the Eastern part.
The Eastern part was already occupied, as you remember.
The Russians had taken away a chunk of--
--by agreement with German.
The Germans took the-- the-- the-- the-- the Western side of Poland, chunk of Poland.
And the Russians took the big side of-- with Lviv, which has become now Ukrainian capital.
And all the young people able who could run away by trains to the Russian-occupied part of Poland.
There was Russia.
They were not afraid to be exterminated.
They went there to-- many of them perished on the way.
Many of them got ill.
Many of them got lost and all kinds of things.
Those who came-- went first-- my husband ran to Lviv, because his parents were in Lviv, and he already worked in Warsaw.
My sister ran to-- to with friends.
She ran to-- She ran to-- to-- to-- first to Bialystok.
Bialystok was on the border between Lithuania.

And then through various stories, which I hope she will recall herself, various, various stories, through the forest, and through the people who guided them, to giving them money, all kinds of things.
Encountered-- encountering Russian police who wanted to shoot them.
Arrived in Kovno, Lithuania.
Lithuania at that time was already under Russia.
And shortly afterwards, the Germans re-entered Lithuania from the ghetto, and liquidated all the Jews.
And there's the famous book, there is a Kovno-- there is a ghetto story of Kovno, of Lithuania, even performed in the-- in the-- in the theater.
And they were very nervous.
They were refugees.
And they said, they don't want to stay in Russia-- in Kovno, in Lithuania.
They want to first-- they didn't know how.
And by extremely lucky chance, there was the firm, you know, the firm called Phillips, a Dutch firm.
A young man who worked there-- and word got round-- I couldn't say that exactly.
But the word got around, even Dr.
Martin Gilbert writes about it in his book of Holocaust.
The-- they-- they-- I suddenly don't remember what I was saying.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Yeah.
No.
That was-- yes, and it was.
And the-- and the-- and the Polish-- yes, no, no, I was telling about the Phillips.
So one of Philips' representatives said that should the Jewish people obtain a transit visa to-- to Japan--
--the Dutch will give them entry to-- to the Dutch Curacao, to Dutch Indies in Curacao.
Yes.
